metadata.title: Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model?

abstract: Validation is at the heart of methodological discussions about topic modeling. The authors argue that validation based on human reading hinges on distinctive words and readers' labeling of a topic, and it overlooks the probability of conflicting results from semantically similar models, such as regressions or other methods. This runs counter to the presumption that topic modeling can reveal features of documents that have some measurable association with social aspects outside the text. The authors develop a similar topic identifying procedure to verify that semantically similar solutions yield similar results in further analysis. The authors argue that future validations of topic modeling must consider such procedures.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: When using a topic model, researchers often find it necessary to label the topics for a better understanding of the model's content. This involves examining distinguishing words within each topic. These distinguishing words typically consist of the top m words or FREX (frequency and exclusivity) words for each topic. Researchers then analyze the semantic relationships among these words to derive a suitable label for the topic with reference to representative documents (Grimmer et al. 2022;Nelson 2020). These labels serve as representations of the content within topics. Using these labels, researchers can uncover intriguing patterns and make discoveries about previously unnoticed trends in the corpus.

Often guided by the semantic interpretation of topics, researchers have embraced topic models as measurement tools. Quantitative metrics associated with topics, such as b and g values, can be leveraged to construct variables for subsequent analyses across documents. This may involve investigating relationships between topic proportions in documents and meta-variables such as authors or dates, or testing hypotheses related to these variables.

Topic models are commonly estimated using the expectation-maximization algorithm. However, because of the intrinsic challenges of navigating nonconvex optimization issues associated with these models, the algorithm tends to produce different solutions in various runs, contingent on the initialization point. In simpler terms, given the corpus and hyperparameters such as k, the expectation-maximization algorithm may identify multiple local solutions, a phenomenon known as the "multimodality problem" (Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2016).

Given the multimodality problem, researchers often struggle to achieve a balance between semantic fit and statistical fit. Central to finding this equilibrium is the validation process. Common validation methods involve facial assessments, such as presenting word clouds or identifying distinguishing terms and related labels. More tailored approaches include close reading of the text, comparing it with human coding of the documents, and mapping the topics to external events (Grimmer and Stewart 2013;Grimmer et al. 2022;Quinn et al. 2010). Validation incorporates information beyond the model to assess the model's capability in addressing specific research questions.

A critical aspect often overlooked in the discourse on validating topic models, however, is whether, once a specific topic model has been validated as a discovery tool through human reading, it can be considered a reliable tool for measurement.

Consider the following scenario. A researcher estimates a topic model with k topics and obtains an output x 1 . Then, they use their expertise to label these topics, and they validate this model through reading the original texts. They confirm the existence of related subject matter in these texts. Running the model with the same value of k for i À 1 additional iterations, they obtain outputs x 2 , :::x i . By examining the top m terms of outputs x 1 to x i , they observe a consistent reappearance of the top m terms across t vectors (t k) among the k topics in many of these i À 1 outputs. Interpreting this consistency as an indication that these t topics persist across various local solutions, they use the t topics from the output of x 1 to conduct a statistical test. The results reveal a positive correlation between the proportions of these topics and the political identity of the document authors. The researcher thus concludes that authors' political identities influence the topics addressed in their documents.

Is this result validated? Given that the t topics consistently reappear in every output of i topic models, it suggests the output of x 1 is comparable with the rest. With a sufficiently large i, the selection seems to be unbiased. The labeling process appears robust as validation has been conducted. On the surface, all aspects seem well supported.

Our analysis, however, shows that validating a topic model through human reading does not necessarily guarantee its effectiveness as a measurement tool. "Human reading" here includes both reading distinguishing words of the topics and close reading of the text. Two outputs of the same topic model algorithms might appear very similar when analyzing distinguishing words in some of their topics. These topics might also share very similar representative documents. However, running a linear regression with the g values (or other measurements constructed on the basis of the model output) from either may yield different results. Coefficients can shift from being significant to nonsignificant or even change signs while remaining significant. Topics perceived as similar by humans may exhibit opposite relationships with other variables. This contradicts a common objective of using topic modeling as a measurement tool in social sciences: to unveil document features that have measurable associations with social elements beyond the text. If validations based on human reading cannot distinguish between outputs that may lead to opposing results in further analyses, further refinement is needed.

To address this issue, we propose the similar topics identifying procedure (STIP), a sensitivity testing procedure. This procedure aims to simulate how human reading perceives two solutions to be similar. The fundamental idea behind STIP is straightforward: it posits that topics that are semantically similar across local solutions should behave similarly as measurements. STIP consists of four steps. First, after selecting an initial reference model, i local solutions are estimated with the same hyperparameters but different initializations. Second, among the i local solutions, semantically similar solutions to the reference model are identified, resulting in a set of n solutions. Third, an examination is conducted to determine whether the topics from these n local solutions share similar contexts with the topics in the reference model. Finally, the same downstream statistical inferences are applied to the n similar local solutions as those conducted with the reference model.

To demonstrate the effectiveness of STIP, we replicate the model presented in Fligstein, Brundage, and Schultz (2017) (hereafter FBS 2017). We show that using seemingly similar solutions from one topic model can result in substantially different outcomes in subsequent analyses. Following the preprocessing steps outlined by FBS (2017), we configure a topic model with the same hyperparameters as in the original article and run it 200 times with different initializations, yielding 200 solutions. From these solutions, we select ones that are semantically similar to the model outlined in FBS (2017) on the basis of distinguishing words. Using FBS's (2017) methodology, we construct measurements and run the same statistical test on them. The findings reveal significant differences in results from the same test across these semantically similar solutions. This suggests that validation based on human reading, although appearing convincing, does not necessarily establish a stable relationship between text features and other social phenomena.

The rest of the article is structured as follows: (1) We first review how topic modeling is used in social sciences, with special attention to the multimodality issue and the importance of validation. (2) We next note what is left out by the current discussion around validation, especially human-reading-based validations. (3) We outline the basic idea and procedure of STIP. (4) Next, we illustrate the use of STIP on the basis of a replication study of FBS (2017). ( 5) Finally, we summarize our findings and engage in a broader discussion of the use of STIP within computational social sciences.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: Topic modeling is now an indispensable tool for sociologists. Quinn et al. (2010) first used topic modeling in political science for document clustering, and Grimmer and Stewart (2013) presented a general framework for incorporating it into social science inquiry. DiMaggio and Blei facilitated the integration of latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic models into sociology, marked by the 2013 Poetics special issue (Mohr et al. 2020). The approach has been adopted widely across political science, sociology, organizational studies, communication studies, and even humanities and demography (Baden et al. 2022;Buurma 2015;Chakrabarti and Frye 2017;Goldstone and Underwood 2014;Grimmer and Stewart 2013;Mohr and Bogdanov 2013).

Researchers have used topic modeling to analyze various materials, including newspaper articles, academic journals, meeting transcripts, policy documents, and online content (DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei 2013;Fligstein et al. 2017;Goldstone and Underwood 2014;Karell and Freedman 2019;Marshall 2013;Mohr et al. 2013;Pardo-Guerra 2020). Its user-friendly nature, 1 capacity to handle large data sets, and the promise of improved transparency have significantly contributed to its popularity (Edelmann and Mohr 2018;Mohr and Bogdanov 2013;Mohr et al. 2020).

Topic models serve two primary purposes: discovery and measurement (Grimmer et al. 2022). These purposes are often intertwined. As a tool for discovery, topic models offer a semiautomatic method to delve into the structure of texts with minimal human intervention and unveil underlying structures (DiMaggio 2015; Grimmer and King 2011;Grimmer et al. 2022). These structures are both interpretable by humans and theoretically intriguing. Such a structure is encapsulated by "the topics, per-document topic distributions, and the per-document per-word topic assignments" estimated by the model (Blei 2012:79).

For instance, in one of the earliest applications in sociology, Mohr et al. (2013) estimated 15 topic models to describe a corpus of 11 U.S. "national security strategy" documents spanning 1990 to 2010. They identified the top 10 words for each topic and assigned labels accordingly. They suggested that these topics captured scenes where actors took action. For example, they highlighted the topic of terrorism and demonstrated that although this topic did not diffuse to more parts of the documents 62
Sociological Methodology 55(1)

after 9/11, the "actors" and "act" within this topic appeared in various scenes. Nelson (2020) summarizes this approach as "computational grounded theory." In the initial step, known as "pattern detection," researchers use unsupervised learning techniques, such as topic modeling, to uncover unknown patterns. Identifying these patterns marks the beginning rather than the end of the research; the model results require further refinement and clarification through "pattern refinement." These findings should then be corroborated by other formal methods (Nelson 2020). Topic modeling has been repurposed as a tool for measurement and applied in statistical inference to test hypotheses or validate patterns (Grimmer et al. 2022). Such analyses go beyond distinguishing words from the output; additional information is incorporated. For example, Karell and Freedman (2019) estimated a 10-topic model. They labeled seven of these topics and grouped them into two sets, associated with "subversion" and "reversion," two interpretations of radicalism. They calculated the ratio between the proportions of these two groups, constructing rhetoric ratio scores. The ratio score serves as the dependent variable in fractional logistic regression. Notably, a significant portion of the information used in this process, such as the g coefficients, constitutes new information that has not been fully used in labeling topics. 2 Yet researchers often overlook the fact that different information is used for different purposes. When topic models are used for discovery, the essence of their use lies in finding intriguing topics. Consequently, labeling topics becomes crucial. Labeling relies on interpretations of distinguishing words in a given context (Grimmer et al. 2022). These distinguishing words are ranked on the basis of statistics such as b coefficients, and a discerning reader interprets them in the context of the documents with the highest topic weights (Grimmer and Stewart 2013;Ying, Montgomery, and Stewart 2022). When topic models are repurposed for measurement, labeling remains important to convince readers the concepts being measured are appropriate. Additionally, measurement uses information such as the b and g matrices. In essence, a topic is represented as a contextualized ordered set in discovery, but it is represented in numeric form in measurement. This inconsistency presents a major obstacle for validation, especially considering the key problem of multimodality in topic models. We delve into this in the following section.

Labeling relies on interpretations of distinguishing words in a given context (Grimmer et al. 2022). These distinguishing words are ranked on the basis of statistics such as b coefficients, and a discerning reader interprets them in the context of the documents with the highest topic weights (Grimmer and Stewart 2013;Ying, Montgomery, and Stewart 2022). When topic models are repurposed for measurement, labeling remains important to convince readers the concepts being measured are appropriate. Additionally, measurement uses information such as the b and g matrices. In essence, a topic is represented as a contextualized ordered set in discovery, but it is represented in numeric form in measurement. This inconsistency presents a major obstacle for validation, especially considering the key problem of multimodality in topic models. We delve into this in the following section.
sections.preview_head: Topic modeling is now an indispensable tool for sociologists. Quinn et al. (2010) first used topic modeling in political science for document clustering, and Grimmer and Stewart (2013) presented a general framework for incorporating it into social science inquiry. DiMaggio and Blei facilitated the integration of latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic models into sociology, marked by the 2013 Poetics special issue (Mohr et al. 2020).

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: Almost all topic models operate on a probabilistic basis: "It specifies a simple probabilistic procedure by which documents can be generated" (Steyvers and Griffiths 2007:427). Estimation of topic models involves a stochastic process, and numerous hyperparameters must be determined by humans. Most prominent among these is the number of topics, denoted as k. Both preprocessing and hyperparameters introduce variations in the final outputs (Denny and Spirling 2018). Different solutions may yield different interpretations, and the solution that appears most sensible may not represent the global solution. Indeed, researchers acknowledge a trade-off between statistical fit and semantic fit (Chang et al. 2009). Achieving this delicate balance requires human judgment (DiMaggio 2015;Grimmer, Roberts, and Stewart 2021;Roberts et al. 2016). Relying solely on optimizing an objective function, such as perplexity or topic coherence scores, to select a model solution is often impractical in real-world scenarios. In essence, as Grimmer and Stewart (2013:270) noted, "there is no globally best method for automated text analysis" such as topic models.

Even when the preprocessing and hyperparameters are given, topic models fall short of stability compared with other common statistical models in social sciences (Grimmer et al. 2022). Given that the space of the document-term matrix is sparse and the objective function is usually nonconvex, the estimation proves highly sensitive to the initial starting values. Each individual run of the same model is likely to result in a local solution, with only a slim chance of it being the global solution. This phenomenon is known as the multimodality issue (Roberts et al. 2016).

Given this challenge, validation is crucial when using topic models, a concern that has existed since these models were first introduced to the social sciences (Grimmer et al. 2022;Grimmer and Stewart 2013). 3 As relying solely on statistical fits proves insufficient for model selection, researchers must introduce external information to substantiate their choices and strengthen the connection between the model and the concept at hand. Validation can take various forms, encompassing "careful reading of the documents, codebooks with category definitions, and/or application-specific criteria" (Grimmer et al. 2021:405).

Facial validation, in which distinguishing words of topics, and their labels, are presented to readers, is the most prevalent form of validation. This process allows readers to independently assess whether the "quality of knowledge" acquired through the topic models is robust.

Customized, or bespoke, validation is often desired. Bespoke validation can manifest in two subforms: validations based on human reading and predictive validation represented by hypothesis validation.

First, bespoke validations based on human reading may involve close reading. Researchers read documents with a high weight of certain topics carefully to ensure the assigned label makes sense. For example, Karell and Freedman (2019) not only presented the top terms of the topics but also provided snippets of documents with significant weights of their key topics to substantiate their interpretations. In another case, FBS (2017) dedicated the second part of their analysis to a close reading of conference transcripts, essentially supporting the discovery of larger frameworks.

It is important to clarify what we mean by "reading" here. In this kind of validation, the reading is under the guidance of the model's results, instead of relational reading of the entirety of the corpus, as might be assumed in the humanistic tradition (Nelson 2020). Such guided close reading is limited in its granularity, because "language in general is quite noun-heavy (particularly after stop words are removed)," and topic models tend to better capture subject matter than a text's more subtle properties such as style and tone (Grimmer et al. 2022:220). 4 In guided close reading, researchers often identify and confirm the discussion's topic. As this validation method mirrors a common human coding task, it can be scaled by creating a formal codebook for human coding. The codebook enables researchers to systematically compare topic model outcomes with those of human reading (Grimmer et al. 2022;Nelson et al. 2021).

The other form of bespoke validation assesses predictive validity. This validation is founded on the idea that specific relationships can be identified between the topic model and external information. Hypothesis validation is a common form of predictive validation. For example, researchers can map the prevalence of topics over time, and the variations in topics should reflect the influence of external events (Grimmer et al. 2022:224). Quinn et al. (2010) estimated a topic model on speeches on the U.S. House of Representatives floor and mapped the number of words associated with "symbolic [remembrance-military]" and "defense [use of force]" topics over time. They discovered that events such as 9/11 and the Iraq War coincided with a surge in related topics. This further increases researchers' confidence in their models.

In the absence of customized bespoke validation, researchers also can turn to human evaluation of the topic quality (Grimmer et al. 2022). Ying et al. (2022), for example, enhanced Chang et al.' (2009) implementation of term intrusion and topic intrusion tests by offering a crowdsourced interface. Nevertheless, bespoke validation remains the gold standard. 5 The disparity between using topic modeling for discovery and using it for measurement introduces additional complexities to the validation process. Most of the validation approaches mentioned above hinge on distinguishing words. In close reading, researchers need to build a connection between distinguishing words and the original text (Nelson 2020); other approaches assess the coherence of these distinguishing words or determine whether distinguishing words reflect the theme of the context (Chang et al. 2009;Grimmer and King 2011;Ying et al. 2022).

Once a topic model is validated to be effective for discovering topics, it is often presumed to be equally valid as a tool for measuring those topics. Consequently, prior discussions on the suitability of topic modeling has centered on instability (Denny and Spirling 2018;Grimmer et al. 2022). In other words, each run appears to yield different results, and their recurrence is improbable. To enhance stability, the goal is to ensure that topics remain consistent across local solutions (Chuang et al. 2015).

However, semantic stability does not necessarily imply validity. As argued earlier, local solutions of a stable topic may exhibit opposite coefficients when used in downstream statistical inference. The key issue here is that validation based on human interpretation often neglects the importance of the b and g matrices, which play a crucial role in measurement.

In guided close reading, researchers read the original text and confirm a pattern. This pattern is usually a subject matter. In this process, researchers only use the reduced information of these two matrices as ordered sets, that is, distinguishing words and representative documents. The specific values of b and g matrices are left behind. Measurements are usually built on these two matrices. Researchers interpret texts, not numbers. The differences between these bodies of information lead to the final discrepancy. Koltcov, Koltsova, and Nikolenko (2014) touched on this point when they suggested L1 distance might not be a suitable distance measurement between topics, because "the huge 'long tail' of terms with low probabilities that are mostly irrelevant for qualitative analysis may contribute to the level of similarity between topics" (p. 161). The same challenge can arise when repurposing topic models for measurement. Two topics from local models might share identical distinguishing words and representative documents yet yield different measurements. Thus, a model effective for discovery may not necessarily excel in measurement.

This problem can become more pronounced when estimating a large number of topics. As the number of topics increases, a majority might not be labeled because of their perceived theoretical irrelevance. Marshall (2013) categorized certain topics as "uninformative," which might serve as a buffer allowing for greater variation in the b and g matrices. When researchers deem only a few topics analytically important, significant variations in these matrices can occur across local models, yet they may remain unnoticed. Do these "consistent" topics also behave consistently as measurements?

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: What lies at the crux of building a connection between a topic and a validation, especially validation based on human reading, is its semantic content, not the values in the b and g matrices. The latter are almost unreadable to humans. If two topics-referred to as topic A and topic B, derived from different local models-are labeled the same within a similar context, then validation relying on human reading that corroborates topic A should logically extend to topic B. As previously discussed, this extrapolation is especially relevant considering the characteristics of topic models, which result in human interpretation of these models typically being confined to confirming or denying the presence of specific subject matter in the original texts.

The basic idea of the STIP is to produce a sufficient number of models within the model space that can be regarded as semantically interchangeable and supported by the same set of validations, and then test whether these models provide consistent measurements for the research purpose.

We assume the researchers have found a meaningful and validated initial reference model. Then, STIP is introduced as a four-step procedure. The first step is surveying. i local solutions are estimated with the same hyperparameters as the reference model but with different initializations. To produce a sufficient number of semantically interchangeable models, i should be relatively large, typically in the hundreds. The second step is alignment. Among these i local solutions, semantically similar solutions to the reference model are identified. In this step, we apply a semantic similarity measurement between two topics on the basis of their distinguishing words. We will explain the aligning strategy later. This step produces a set of n semantically interchangeable solutions. Because researchers read a topic within a context of texts, the third step is context checking. The researchers check whether the aligned topics from these n local solutions share contextual similarities with the topics in the reference model. We check the overlapping of top documents of two aligned topics to ensure the two topics are not only composed of similar distinguishing words but are also based on two comparable sets of texts. The final step is robustness assessment. The same downstream statistical inferences are applied to the n similar local solutions as those conducted with the reference model.

To identify local solutions that are semantically similar to the reference model in the alignment step, we introduce the average Jaccard index (AJI) as a measure of the similarity between the two topics. The Jaccard similarity index measures the similarity between two sets by comparing the members of their top m terms to determine the percentage of shared terms. AJI calculates these indices for the top w terms as w ranges from 1 to m and then averages these values.
In other words, for two topics in the form of two ordered lists, R i and R j , each including m top terms, AJI is defined as
where

Here, m refers to the length of the window a researcher uses to determine the semantic contents of a topic. In prior work, this number usually varies from 10 to 50. R i, w here refers to the first w elements of the ordered list R i . One advantage of using AJI instead of another measurement is that AJI considers both the number of overlapped distinguishing terms and their sequence. Compared with other widely used distance measurements such as L1 and L2 distances, AJI is more similar to how a human reader approaches a topic.

Consider the three topics represented by their top five terms in Table 1. In this example, m equals 5. Local topics A and B both have four terms that overlap with the reference topic, the only different term is "employment." The sequence of top terms in topic A is more similar to the reference topic, with the first four in the same order. This similarity is reflected in the calculation of AJI. In calculating the distance, w increases iteratively from 1 to 5, and we will first calculate the Jaccard similarity index of the first w elements between two topics. We add them and calculate the average. The AJI between reference topic and topic A is 1 5 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 4=6 ð Þ = 0:933; the AJI between reference topic and topic B is 1 5 1 + 1=3 + 1=5 + 3=5 + 4=6 ð Þ = 0:56. For a specific local model, we match its topics with the topics from the reference model in all possible ways and calculate their AJIs, that is, every possible topic-to- topic match. Then we search for a particular alignment to make the means of AJIs in this alignment maximum in all possible alignments. We use the Hungarian algorithm, which is a combinatorial optimization algorithm that addresses the assignment problems (Roberts et al. 2016). 6 After aligning the local model and reference model, a threshold is needed to determine when AJI is high enough to consider the two topics in question as "semantically interchangeable." With this threshold, we can filter out local models that could be seen as the replicated ones of the reference model. A full-replicated model has all the aligned topics that pass the chosen threshold; a key-topic-replicated model has all the analytically important topics that pass the threshold. A practical problem arises here: a high AJI indicates that two topics are semantically close and likely to be interpreted as the same, but how high is high enough? This kind of standard is usually determined by academic convention and specific research needs. The criterion we adopted in our showcase is relatively arbitrary. We suggest that researchers begin with a relatively loose threshold and then gradually increase it. This strategy allows one to observe whether model performance changes as AJI increases and local models become even more similar to the reference model.

In context checking, we calculate the number of overlapped documents between the top documents of the aligned topic in the local and reference models. 7 Our objective is to ensure that topics with high semantic similarity in terms of distinguishing terms also share similar contexts. This is crucial because, during the reading and labeling of a topic, researchers often consult documents with significant weights in specific topics. Therefore, context checking replicates this process to ensure the interpretation of the two aligned topics is closely matched.

Alignment and context checking cover various situations in labeling topics, from model-based ones to document-based ones described by Grimmer et al. (2022). Instead of using top terms, researchers can also use FREX terms in the alignment, and choose other parameters to compare the context of aligned topics.

Through the steps of surveying, alignment, and context checking, we can identify local models that are semantically similar to the reference model. In other words, if researchers consider the reference model to be suitable for their work, any of the local models generated through the scholarly process could be seen as equivalent. They would interpret results from these local models in a similar way to the reference model, and their interpretation would be reinforced by the same human-reading-based validation. If they conduct any statistical inference on the basis of the reference model, they should obtain similar results with these local models. Therefore, the final step of STIP is to scale up the same statistical inference for both the reference model and selected local models. We call this robustness assessment.

STIP shares a key idea from multiple threads in recent methodological discussion: in answering social science questions, one should use multiple models instead of one to address uncertainty related to models and measurements (Chuang et al. 2015;Knox, Lucas, and Cho 2022;Mun ˜oz and Young 2018;Young and Holsteen 2017). Specific to the application of topic models, Chuang et al. (2015) devised an interactive way for researchers to survey the entire model space instead of only reporting one reference model. Roberts et al. (2016) and Wilkerson and Casas (2017), from different points, both suggested that one can examine the robustness of a topic across multiple models. Wilkerson and Casas further argued that "similarity of estimates of topic emphasis across different models is an important type of validation" (p. 539). On the basis of this general approach, STIP highlights the connection between the bespoke validation and the model selected. Instead of representing the entire modeling space, it focuses on a subarea that a certain validation supports. 8 In the next section, we use a replication of FBS (2017) as a showcase for STIP.

In this step, we apply a semantic similarity measurement between two topics on the basis of their distinguishing words. We will explain the aligning strategy later. This step produces a set of n semantically interchangeable solutions. Because researchers read a topic within a context of texts, the third step is context checking. The researchers check whether the aligned topics from these n local solutions share contextual similarities with the topics in the reference model. We check the overlapping of top documents of two aligned topics to ensure the two topics are not only composed of similar distinguishing words but are also based on two comparable sets of texts. The final step is robustness assessment. The same downstream statistical inferences are applied to the n similar local solutions as those conducted with the reference model.

To identify local solutions that are semantically similar to the reference model in the alignment step, we introduce the average Jaccard index (AJI) as a measure of the similarity between the two topics. The Jaccard similarity index measures the similarity between two sets by comparing the members of their top m terms to determine the percentage of shared terms. AJI calculates these indices for the top w terms as w ranges from 1 to m and then averages these values.

In other words, for two topics in the form of two ordered lists, R i and R j , each including m top terms, AJI is defined as

where

Here, m refers to the length of the window a researcher uses to determine the semantic contents of a topic. In prior work, this number usually varies from 10 to 50. R i, w here refers to the first w elements of the ordered list R i . One advantage of using AJI instead of another measurement is that AJI considers both the number of overlapped distinguishing terms and their sequence. Compared with other widely used distance measurements such as L1 and L2 distances, AJI is more similar to how a human reader approaches a topic.

Consider the three topics represented by their top five terms in Table 1. In this example, m equals 5. Local topics A and B both have four terms that overlap with the reference topic, the only different term is "employment." The sequence of top terms in topic A is more similar to the reference topic, with the first four in the same order. This similarity is reflected in the calculation of AJI. In calculating the distance, w increases iteratively from 1 to 5, and we will first calculate the Jaccard similarity index of the first w elements between two topics. We add them and calculate the average. The AJI between reference topic and topic A is 1 5 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 4=6 ð Þ = 0:933; the AJI between reference topic and topic B is 1 5 1 + 1=3 + 1=5 + 3=5 + 4=6 ð Þ = 0:56. For a specific local model, we match its topics with the topics from the reference model in all possible ways and calculate their AJIs, that is, every possible topic-to- topic match. Then we search for a particular alignment to make the means of AJIs in this alignment maximum in all possible alignments. We use the Hungarian algorithm, which is a combinatorial optimization algorithm that addresses the assignment problems (Roberts et al. 2016). 6 After aligning the local model and reference model, a threshold is needed to determine when AJI is high enough to consider the two topics in question as "semantically interchangeable." With this threshold, we can filter out local models that could be seen as the replicated ones of the reference model. A full-replicated model has all the aligned topics that pass the chosen threshold; a key-topic-replicated model has all the analytically important topics that pass the threshold. A practical problem arises here: a high AJI indicates that two topics are semantically close and likely to be interpreted as the same, but how high is high enough? This kind of standard is usually determined by academic convention and specific research needs. The criterion we adopted in our showcase is relatively arbitrary. We suggest that researchers begin with a relatively loose threshold and then gradually increase it. This strategy allows one to observe whether model performance changes as AJI increases and local models become even more similar to the reference model.

In context checking, we calculate the number of overlapped documents between the top documents of the aligned topic in the local and reference models. 7 Our objective is to ensure that topics with high semantic similarity in terms of distinguishing terms also share similar contexts. This is crucial because, during the reading and labeling of a topic, researchers often consult documents with significant weights in specific topics. Therefore, context checking replicates this process to ensure the interpretation of the two aligned topics is closely matched.

Alignment and context checking cover various situations in labeling topics, from model-based ones to document-based ones described by Grimmer et al. (2022). Instead of using top terms, researchers can also use FREX terms in the alignment, and choose other parameters to compare the context of aligned topics.

Through the steps of surveying, alignment, and context checking, we can identify local models that are semantically similar to the reference model. In other words, if researchers consider the reference model to be suitable for their work, any of the local models generated through the scholarly process could be seen as equivalent. They would interpret results from these local models in a similar way to the reference model, and their interpretation would be reinforced by the same human-reading-based validation. If they conduct any statistical inference on the basis of the reference model, they should obtain similar results with these local models. Therefore, the final step of STIP is to scale up the same statistical inference for both the reference model and selected local models. We call this robustness assessment.

STIP shares a key idea from multiple threads in recent methodological discussion: in answering social science questions, one should use multiple models instead of one to address uncertainty related to models and measurements (Chuang et al. 2015;Knox, Lucas, and Cho 2022;Mun ˜oz and Young 2018;Young and Holsteen 2017). Specific to the application of topic models, Chuang et al. (2015) devised an interactive way for researchers to survey the entire model space instead of only reporting one reference model. Roberts et al. (2016) and Wilkerson and Casas (2017), from different points, both suggested that one can examine the robustness of a topic across multiple models. Wilkerson and Casas further argued that "similarity of estimates of topic emphasis across different models is an important type of validation" (p. 539). On the basis of this general approach, STIP highlights the connection between the bespoke validation and the model selected. Instead of representing the entire modeling space, it focuses on a subarea that a certain validation supports. 8 In the next section, we use a replication of FBS (2017) as a showcase for STIP.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: FBS ( 2017) is a relatively early and classic example of applying topic modeling in the sociological context, combining the purposes of discovery and statistical inferences. FBS investigated whether the epistemological frames of the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) made it slow to recognize the systematic risk underlying the financial system before the 2008 financial crisis.

Analyzing 73 FOMC meeting transcripts between 2000 and 2008 using the LDA topic model, FBS (2017) detected two major frames, macroeconomic and financial, in a 15-topic solution. They argued that the macroeconomic frame dominated the FOMC for most of the time before 2008. FBS associated the frames with FOMC members' backgrounds, hypothesizing that financial frames would be more prevalent among members with a background in private banking. This idea is hypothesis 2 in FBS, and we refer to it as the private banking hypothesis. FBS supported this hypothesis via a permutation test on the measurement constructed using the topic model's result. They randomly permuted profession codings 10,000 times, and they showed that members with private banking backgrounds were significantly more likely to adopt financial frames than were members without such backgrounds.

FBS (2017) thus concluded that the FOMC failed to anticipate the risks before the 2008 crisis because of its macroeconomic frame, which, in turn, was a result of its membership structure. FBS further strengthened the validation of their conclusion by closely examining a few FOMC meetings. They observed how specific participants in these meetings interpreted market situations and how their backgrounds interacted with their frames. This validation process is a typical case of the bespoke validation we discussed.

We selected FBS (2017) as a showcase for three reasons. First, the article is ideal for a replication study. The corpus, the FOMC transcripts, is publicly accessible, and FBS clearly explained how they preprocessed the material. Also, the article provides detailed results of the topic model outputs, such as the top 30 distinguishing words calculated by "word weights" and the time-variant trend of each topic. Second, the article represents the typical practice of applying topic models in sociology. The LDA model is probably the single most used model in topic modeling, with a few variants developed on the basis of that model (e.g., the structural topic model). FBS used the method not only as a discovery tool but also as an instrument for measurement in statistical inferences. Third, and more important, FBS provided very good validation for the model they identified; they are experts on this topic. The second part of their article provides a close reading of the corpus, and it can be seen as a bespoke validation based on human reading. Additionally, FBS mapped the proportion of the topics along the timeline with major events. This is a common practice of predictive validation.

As we will demonstrate, with a few models semantically interchangeable with the model presented in the article, our statistical test leads to some different results, generating opportunities to challenge FBS's (2017) conclusions. We believe the main issue is not that FBS misused topic modeling but that it is important to be clear about when topic modeling is used for finding new insights versus when it is used for precise measurement. Making this distinction clear is the main goal of the STIP method. The next section shows our application of STIP using the replication as an example. 9

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: We replicated the original preprocessing and successfully generated an identical corpus. 10 In surveying, we executed the LDA topic model 200 times using the same parameters but different initializations. The only variation is the use of a different package for model estimation and a different operating system. Because FBS (2017) provided the top 30 terms for each topic, we considered these terms in each replicated model and calculated the AJI between each model and the reference model. Note that FBS did not use b values to order the terms, unlike many other researchers. Instead, FBS introduced a new index, denoted as X in their codes, which considers both the distribution of terms across topics and the significance of terms within the corpus. We replicate this approach to generate the top terms for each model. As mentioned earlier, the alignment process requires a standard to determine when an AJI is sufficiently high to indicate that two topics are semantically interchangeable. In this case, we set the standard on the basis of two criteria: an AJI larger than 0.15 and overlapping terms more than 10. Although this standard may seem somewhat arbitrary, we examine the "landmark words" that FBS used in their labeling process to make sense of a topic (see below). The goal of this step is to ensure the topics are highly similar in human interpretation. 11 Among our 200 attempts, 38 are considered fully replicated models, as all 15 topics aligned pass the replication threshold. We compare the results with the labeling process in FBS (2017). In explaining the labeling process, FBS singled out certain of the 30 top distinguishing words to support their decisions. We call these the landmark words, and we compare them with distinguishing words in the 38 models that pass the threshold. The results are presented in Table 2. All topics, except housing, have more than half of these landmark words found in these models. The replicated models cover most of the top five distinguishing words of the model in FBS. The average AJI of these models is also much higher than the threshold we set.

When applying topic models, researchers usually place more analytic weight on certain topics that are theoretically relevant and interesting (Buurma 2015;DiMaggio 2015;Grimmer and Stewart 2013;Marshall 2013). Not all topics in a topic model are equally important. Following this, we relax our filter to include models in which all eight "key" topics meet the two-rule standard. FBS (2017) used these eight topics to differentiate financial frames (bank liquidity, financial markets, housing bubble, policy response) and microeconomic frames (inflation, productivity, employment, weakness). Among our 200 attempts, 112 models meet this standard. We refer to these models as "key similar" models.

To further demonstrate that the 38 successful replications are semantically interchangeable with the reference model, we consider the topic "energy," which has a relatively low AJI and overall overlapped words compared with other topics in these replicated models. We randomly selected 10 models out of the 38 replications. Table 3 presents the top 30 distinguishing words and related b coefficients of the topic "energy."FBS (2017:893) explained their labeling process as follows: "This topic captures concerns about rising oil and gas prices, in large part dealing with supply problems caused by Hurricane Katrina (gasoline, energy, disruptions) and the effects of the Iraq War (geopolitical, military)." Most replicated models include the three landmark words associated with "supply problems caused by Hurricane Katrina," as well as "hurricane" and "Katrina." Of the 10 models, six replicate both aspects of Hurricane Katrina and the Iraq War; four models only capture one aspect of them. If FBS had achieved one of these models in their attempts, it would be reasonable for them to label it in the same way.

Tables 4 and 5 show one of these fully replicated models; this model, from the robustness assessment, replicates the original permutation results and supports the hypothesis that "the finance and banking frame was more prevalent among members of the FOMC who had a professional background in the private banking sector than among those who did not," which FBS (2017:885) called their hypothesis 2 and we refer to as the private banking hypothesis.

We compare the results with the labeling process in FBS (2017). In explaining the labeling process, FBS singled out certain of the 30 top distinguishing words to support their decisions. We call these the landmark words, and we compare them with distinguishing words in the 38 models that pass the threshold. The results are presented in Table 2. All topics, except housing, have more than half of these landmark words found in these models. The replicated models cover most of the top five distinguishing words of the model in FBS. The average AJI of these models is also much higher than the threshold we set.

When applying topic models, researchers usually place more analytic weight on certain topics that are theoretically relevant and interesting (Buurma 2015;DiMaggio 2015;Grimmer and Stewart 2013;Marshall 2013). Not all topics in a topic model are equally important. Following this, we relax our filter to include models in which all eight "key" topics meet the two-rule standard. FBS (2017) used these eight topics to differentiate financial frames (bank liquidity, financial markets, housing bubble, policy response) and microeconomic frames (inflation, productivity, employment, weakness). Among our 200 attempts, 112 models meet this standard. We refer to these models as "key similar" models.

To further demonstrate that the 38 successful replications are semantically interchangeable with the reference model, we consider the topic "energy," which has a relatively low AJI and overall overlapped words compared with other topics in these replicated models. We randomly selected 10 models out of the 38 replications. Table 3 presents the top 30 distinguishing words and related b coefficients of the topic "energy."FBS (2017:893) explained their labeling process as follows: "This topic captures concerns about rising oil and gas prices, in large part dealing with supply problems caused by Hurricane Katrina (gasoline, energy, disruptions) and the effects of the Iraq War (geopolitical, military)." Most replicated models include the three landmark words associated with "supply problems caused by Hurricane Katrina," as well as "hurricane" and "Katrina." Of the 10 models, six replicate both aspects of Hurricane Katrina and the Iraq War; four models only capture one aspect of them. If FBS had achieved one of these models in their attempts, it would be reasonable for them to label it in the same way.

Tables 4 and 5 show one of these fully replicated models; this model, from the robustness assessment, replicates the original permutation results and supports the hypothesis that "the finance and banking frame was more prevalent among members of the FOMC who had a professional background in the private banking sector than among those who did not," which FBS (2017:885) called their hypothesis 2 and we refer to as the private banking hypothesis.
sections.preview_head: We replicated the original preprocessing and successfully generated an identical corpus. 10 In surveying, we executed the LDA topic model 200 times using the same parameters but different initializations. The only variation is the use of a different package for model estimation and a different operating system. Because FBS (2017) provided the top 30 terms for each topic, we considered these terms in each replicated model and calculated the AJI between each model and the reference model.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: In the third step of STIP (context checking), we examine the similarity between the reference and the replicated models. The goal is to determine whether the top documents of two models considered similar exhibit substantial overlap. In our replication, we extract information on the top documents for each topic in FBS (2017) using its replication package. We then compare the top documents of the alternative model with the 38 full replications. For a specific pair of models, we tally the overlapping documents among their top 10 documents, representing the 10 documents with the largest proportion for these topics. Figure 1 depicts the results. With the reasonable exception of the "general" topic, most full replications share seven to nine documents among their top 10. 12 Even for the least stable topic, "employment," 26 of the 38 models share five or six documents with the reference. This implies that two similar topics are not only alike in terms of their distinguishing words but also in the contexts, that is, the top documents, from which they are extracted. Were researchers to interpret these topics with guided close reading, they would read a lot of overlapped documents.

Given that the topic model tends to pick up subject better than other features of a corpus, the researcher is likely to label these topics the same and treat them as semantically equivalent in validations based on human reading.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: The previous steps involve selecting the replicated models that are semantically interchangeable with the reference model and examining their similarity. Like many other researchers using topic modeling, FBS (2017) used the reference model to conduct a statistical inference and suggested that differences in frames existed between FOMC members with financial experience and those without. We infer that, because the replicated models are semantically interchangeable with the reference model, they should yield the same results in the test, as these models are supported by the same set of validations. FBS (2017) asserted that bankers' career paths shape the frameworks they use when contemplating economic situations during meetings. Specifically, FBS's hypothesis 2 (p. 885) contends there was a greater prevalence of the finance and banking frame among FOMC members who had a background in the private banking sector compared with those who did not. To test this hypothesis, FBS introduced an innovative measurement using word weight, that is, the probability of a topic given a word. We adopt this measurement in our replications.

FBS (2017) broke down a meeting into utterances (defined as individual speeches) and assigned 15 topic scores to each utterance by summing corresponding topic probability given a word. These scores were then normalized by the length of the utterance. 13 Ultimately, each speaker had 15 weightings for all the utterances they spoke. FBS suggested these scores can be interpreted as the likelihood a speaker mentioned certain topics during the meeting. They conducted a permutation test by categorizing the utterances on the basis of whether the speaker had private bank experience. In the permutation test, they randomly permuted profession codings 10,000 times. The results provided evidence supporting their private banking hypothesis. For all topics related to the financial crisis (housing bubble, financial markets, bank liquidity, and policy response), they found that speakers with private banking experience had significantly higher topic weightings.

Having identified similar models, we constructed the same word weight measurement using the b coefficients and reran the permutation test on these models. We first ran the test on the 38 models where all topics pass the threshold. We do not exactly replicate FBS's (2017) estimates. These differences are understandable given many other minor differences in model construction, as we note in the online supplement. In this context, readers should not compare our results with the ones presented in FBS but focus on the variation within the graphs. Figure 2 shows the estimates, the AJI with distinguishing words presented in FBS. The shades of the dots indicate whether the related test is significant.

As seen in Figure 2, topics related to financial markets and bank liquidity are relatively stable. Most of the 38 models have positive estimates and are reported as being statistically significant. For housing bubble and policy response, about half the replications fail the permutation test. For most fully replicated models, the topic weakness is significantly positive, and the topic employment is not significant but also positive, both of which, according to FBS (2017), should be microeconomic topics and negative. This suggests there is a substantial chance in any given replication that a model is semantically similar to the reference model but does not support FBS's private banking hypothesis. However, the topic inflation is negatively significant in most models, which is consistent with their private banking hypothesis but not observed in their reference model. These patterns still exist among the 112 "key similar" models with key topics replicated, as Figure 3 shows.

Figure 4 singles out productivity, one of the eight key topics, from Figures 2 and 3. One fully replicated model is significantly negative, consistent with the private banking hypothesis. However, among the 112 "key similar" models, three models report significant positive results, and four models report significant negative results. We thus see that semantically interchangeable results may produce opposite statistical inferences, even when the estimates are statistically significant. This uncertainty is not accidental; a similar pattern exists for objectivity (see Figure 5) among 38 fully replicated models, suggesting that if a hypothesis were proposed around this topic, one might get opposite results across different runs. Table 6 shows distinguishing words of these models and demonstrates they are highly similar.

To further confirm our finding, both productivity and objectivity perform well in context checking. Using the topic objectivity as an example, topics D, E, and f share the same five top documents along the same order. A researcher reading the top five documents closely would conclude that topics D, E, and f are meaningfully the same. We further enhance this check by segmenting these documents into 200-word snippets; we calculate relevance scores for these segments by dividing the number of words in the snippet that match distinguishing words by the total number of stemmed words in the snippet. We select the most relevant segments by ranking these scores. The three topics return largely identical segments. Even when different segments pop out, one can confirm via close reading that the objectivity of policies was an important subject during the FOMC meetings. We present these segments in the online supplement.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: One could argue that temporal trends, as seen in Figure 2 of FBS (2017), provide a visual means to enhance the predictive validity of labeling. If a notable change in a topic aligns with a relevant external event, it can serve as a valuable extraneous test for validating the model (Grimmer and Stewart 2013;Quinn et al. 2010). There are two possible uses of this hypothesis validation: the first is to use the predictive validity of a topic to support the selection of the whole model; the second is that the predictive validity of a topic only supports the selection of that particular topic. We find that both approaches have caveats.

For the first kind of use, the topic of "energy" is a good example. In the reference model, its proportion experiences a significant rise in the second half of 2005, likely because of Hurricane Katrina. The hurricane caused supply shortages and increased oil prices, so the close association of this topic with energy is externally validated. To explore this possibility, we depict the trends of energy from the following models: the original reference model from FBS (2017), a full replication model with the permutation tests consistent with their private banking hypothesis, and "key similar" models that also have an energy topic that passed the replication threshold but failed to fully replicate the result related to the private banking hypothesis. As Figure 6 shows, although these models have different estimates, they all have similar peaks around August, 2005, when Hurricane Katrina occurred. In other words, although this hypothesis validation indicates an association between these topics and energy, it allows for a relatively wide range of possibilities in the downstream statistical tests in other topics.

We further raise caution about this method by depicting the housing bubble topic from all "key similar" models. This topic can be seen as self-validated, given its analytic importance. Similar to FBS (2017), these models also feature a peak in 2005, which is supposed to represent the FOMC meeting on June 29 and 30, 2005, focusing on the possible bubble in the housing market. The trends from the "key similar" models are highly overlapped, making it challenging to visually discern the differences between these topics (see Figure 7). Notably, whether these models would be significant in the permutation test is not predictable via the figure.

In other words, even if topics are semantically interchangeable and validated by external events, they may still have different statistical properties. Therefore, although predictive validity is a reasonable means of validation, it still has some limitations and eyeballing may fail to discern some important differences between models.

sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
sections.header_raw: Toward Improved Validation: Dissecting the Divergence in Measurement
sections.header_norm: toward improved validation dissecting the divergence in measurement
paragraphs: Our research primarily serves as a cautionary note about the need to clarify the different uses of topic modeling for exploration and measurement. In typical practice, researchers read distinguishing words and related representative documents to label topics. When a topic in a local solution of a topic model (topic A) looks quite similar to a topic in another local solution (topic B) and they share a similar set of representative documents, they are interpreted as the same recurring topic. By extension, validating topic A via human reading also validates topic B. Unsuspecting researchers may then use one solution for further analysis. However, our analyses show that topic A and topic B may be labeled the same in a similar context, yet yield opposite results in further analyses. Significant results can become insignificant, and positive results can become negative. This inconsistency implies that the same reading of the model supports different results, indicating the need for improved validation methods.

To illustrate this point, we replicated FBS's (2017) private banking hypothesis, that financial frames were more prevalent among Federal Reserve Board members with a background in private banking, using a procedure we refer to as STIP. Using corpus data obtained from their replication package, we found that solutions that appear similar may lead to conflicting results for certain topics.

To clarify, the issue we demonstrate is not specific to the corpus that FBS (2017) models or their chosen model settings. It is a more general problem rooted in the multimodality issue and how researchers use topic models. As documented in the online supplement, we identify similar issues with corpora used for methodological discussions and with other types of topic models.

The connection between validation and topic models underscores a critical issue in interpreting and applying these models. Topic models tend to extract themes from texts, and close reading guided by these models focuses on these themes. This differs fundamentally from traditional human reading in humanistic interpretative sociology. Instead of viewing the entire corpus as an irreducible whole, close reading to validate topic models is strategically guided in two senses. First, topic models can identify representative texts on the basis of the relative prevalence of topics, allowing a researcher to "'read' and interpret any amount of text without the burden of reading the full text" (Nelson 2020:26). Second, this close reading refers to distinguishing words, which, because language is typically noun heavy, capture subject matter more effectively than other text characteristics (Grimmer et al. 2022). This interpretable representation of the model is seen as equivalent to themes in sociology (Nelson 2020). In short, in validation based on close reading, information from distinguishing words and ranked representative texts plays a pivotal role. Given these conditions, the granularity of such reading is usually confined to checking whether, "when a quote is chosen as an example of something, it is not an outlier but is indeed representative of some theme in the text" (Nelson 2020:26).

When validation is based on model-guided reading, it involves subtleties that can go unnoticed and are beyond the scope of human discernment. This method uses condensed information (i.e., distinguishing words and representative documents), and model outputs used in constructing the measurement may be unreadable to a researcher or ignored, making it challenging to build a solid connection between the semantic representations and model parameters. This can lead to a situation where a single validation might support local models that yield unstable or even conflicting results in downstream analyses.

STIP thus serves as a noise detector, offering researchers a simple and effective means to identify potential issues arising from the gap between model use and researchers' understanding of the model. In the exploratory phase of computational social sciences, researchers often iterate through multiple models. STIP can be a valuable tool in this step, efficiently filtering out models with excessive noise.

More important, STIP enhances the validation process when a model is used as a measurement tool. Our research primarily serves as a cautionary note about the need to clarify the different uses of topic modeling for exploration and measurement. In typical practice, researchers read distinguishing words and related representative documents to label topics. When a topic in a local solution of a topic model (topic A) looks quite similar to a topic in another local solution (topic B) and they share a similar set of representative documents, they are interpreted as the same recurring topic. By extension, validating topic A via human reading also validates topic B. Unsuspecting researchers may then use one solution for further analysis. However, our analyses show that topic A and topic B may be labeled the same in a similar context, yet yield opposite results in further analyses. Significant results can become insignificant, and positive results can become negative. This inconsistency implies that the same reading of the model supports different results, indicating the need for improved validation methods.

To illustrate this point, we replicated FBS's (2017) private banking hypothesis, that financial frames were more prevalent among Federal Reserve Board members with a background in private banking, using a procedure we refer to as STIP. Using corpus data obtained from their replication package, we found that solutions that appear similar may lead to conflicting results for certain topics.

To clarify, the issue we demonstrate is not specific to the corpus that FBS (2017) models or their chosen model settings. It is a more general problem rooted in the multimodality issue and how researchers use topic models. As documented in the online supplement, we identify similar issues with corpora used for methodological discussions and with other types of topic models.

The connection between validation and topic models underscores a critical issue in interpreting and applying these models. Topic models tend to extract themes from texts, and close reading guided by these models focuses on these themes. This differs fundamentally from traditional human reading in humanistic interpretative sociology. Instead of viewing the entire corpus as an irreducible whole, close reading to validate topic models is strategically guided in two senses. First, topic models can identify representative texts on the basis of the relative prevalence of topics, allowing a researcher to "'read' and interpret any amount of text without the burden of reading the full text" (Nelson 2020:26). Second, this close reading refers to distinguishing words, which, because language is typically noun heavy, capture subject matter more effectively than other text characteristics (Grimmer et al. 2022). This interpretable representation of the model is seen as equivalent to themes in sociology (Nelson 2020). In short, in validation based on close reading, information from distinguishing words and ranked representative texts plays a pivotal role. Given these conditions, the granularity of such reading is usually confined to checking whether, "when a quote is chosen as an example of something, it is not an outlier but is indeed representative of some theme in the text" (Nelson 2020:26).

When validation is based on model-guided reading, it involves subtleties that can go unnoticed and are beyond the scope of human discernment. This method uses condensed information (i.e., distinguishing words and representative documents), and model outputs used in constructing the measurement may be unreadable to a researcher or ignored, making it challenging to build a solid connection between the semantic representations and model parameters. This can lead to a situation where a single validation might support local models that yield unstable or even conflicting results in downstream analyses.

STIP thus serves as a noise detector, offering researchers a simple and effective means to identify potential issues arising from the gap between model use and researchers' understanding of the model. In the exploratory phase of computational social sciences, researchers often iterate through multiple models. STIP can be a valuable tool in this step, efficiently filtering out models with excessive noise.

STIP also helps ensure the measurements one constructs do not yield conflicting results among similar local solutions.

However, for optimal effectiveness, these methods must meet certain conditions. First, researchers must choose an appropriate window for interpreting a topic model, that is, an appropriate number of distinguishing terms. In existing research, this number varies from 10 to 50. A shorter window makes it easier to identify topics that lack coherence, but it also discards more information. A longer window makes it more challenging for researchers to provide a coherent label. Second, researchers need to define a well-constructed topic. If a very broad topic label is assigned, as in the case of energy, distinguishing between different local solutions with slight semantic differences becomes more challenging. 14 In both cases, researchers often lack criteria to determine whether their chosen windows and labels are adequate. sections.paper_id: Zhang et al. - 2025 - Can Human Reading Validate a Topic Model
paragraphs: Topic models stand out as a valuable tool in the social sciences, offering a quick summary of a corpus and unveiling its underlying structure. Despite its extensive applications, the methodological discussion lags behind its broader use (DiMaggio 2015). We have moved beyond the phase where we need to advocate for the adoption of computational techniques such as topic models. As the utility of topic modeling becomes increasingly apparent, sociologists must deepen our understanding of methodological caveats and enhance our practices. It is insufficient to merely assert that "there can be no measurement of culture without interpretation" and "interpretation and measurement are . . . intertwined" (Mohr et al. 2020:4). Instead, we should aim to elucidate the intricate relationship between interpretation and measurement, avoiding oversimplification and fostering a more detailed discussion about how they are practically intertwined.

This article adds another layer to the existing discussion about the stability of topic models. Replicating FBS (2017) as a proof of concept, we show that semantically similar solutions could yield conflicting results in statistical inference. Two solutions that seem to be supported by the same validation might provide opposite evidence. Building on recent debates on unsupervised learning (Denny and Spirling 2018;Grimmer et al. 2021), we argue that procedures such as STIP can uncover such conflicting solutions and improve validation. STIP can be a valuable tool in model diagnosis and help researchers increase the transparency of their research.

The significant incorporation of computer-science innovations into the set of methods used in sociology is a prominent trend in the field's progression. Techniques are advancing more rapidly than ever before, and generative artificial intelligence is showing its capacity to transform the discipline. We believe STIP contributes to fostering a new human-algorithm assemblage within the social sciences.

over a year and a half. He opted for a model with 39 topics. He did not label each topic, but he reviewed the top 50 words associated with each topic to ensure coherence. This serves as a foundation for constructing measurements, by transforming the standard deviation of g coefficients of organizations' posts in a sliding time window. 3. Another approach, although less often used, is to seek solutions through an ensemble process. For instance, Chuang et al. (2015) suggested that a single topic model may not capture all perspectives in a corpus. They propose an automated process to fit multiple models on the same corpus, aligning and matching similar topics across local solutions. Consequently, a researcher can easily identify all meaningful topics in the corpus and their cooccurrence. Similarly, Rieger (2020) and Belford and Greene (2020) presented a single model summarizing the model space through either model averaging or an ensemble process. Yet social scientists rarely use this approach. Although Roberts et al. (2016) introduced an improved initialization strategy, they also suggested that it does not yield a global solution but a sufficiently good local solution. 4. Some exceptions stand out. For instance, Nelson (2021) discovered that women's movements across various cities used distinct narrative types: one concrete, the other abstract. However, this divergence, initially observed in topic model results, also reflects differences in the subject of the texts. 5. Ying et al. (2022) discovered that bespoke validation is exceedingly rare in works applying topic models in political science. Among 20 works published in the top political science journals from January 2018 to January 2021, only four pieces included bespoke validation, and five provided information about statistical fits. The situation in sociology is no better. We collected 33 papers using topic models. Among them, 22 pieces state that they conducted bespoke validation via close reading, but only a few present the validation in the main text or appendix. Few of these works provide hypothesis validation; 15 provide some kind of fit statistics. For more information, please refer to the online supplement. 6. The Hungarian algorithm is a common procedure to solve assignment problems such as the one here.

The input of the algorithm is an n-by-n matrix with non-negative values. For its procedures and code example, refer to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hungarian_algorithm. We also discuss alternatives to AJI as distance measurement in the online supplement. 7. Alternatively, one could use AJI here. The pitfall is that the window of top documents is usually much smaller, and AJI will be very dispersed even given slight changes. 8. One could also consider model averaging. It is challenging in the case of topic models, as we are uncertain which models we should average. As mentioned earlier, the replication threshold one sets will not necessarily be an ideal one, and there is no suitable weight we can rely on during model averaging. The current approach to model averaging, as seen in Rieger (2020), is more like finding the most representative model in the modeling space, and this defeats the very purpose of validating the model. Thus, we adhere to the idea of presenting the uncertainty to increase transparency, as suggested by Young and Holsteen (2017) and Knox et al. (2022). 9. Some may wonder whether FBS is a good example given that they estimated a small number of topics on 73 long texts. We argue that these criticisms, at best, lack a solid methodological consensus. Furthermore, the issues we highlight are not unique to this study; they exist in corpus widely used in methodological discussion. We provide more information about this in the online supplement. 10. When we wrote to FBS (2017) for clarification of some technical issues, they generously provided a replication package. We also followed their verbal instructions, and we generated a slightly different corpus. These differences do not affect our argument. We provide more information about this in the online supplement. 11. We add the number of overlapped terms here because we want to filter out topics that have overlapped terms mainly located in the beginning of distinguishing words, resulting in a very high AJI.

This might happen if one has a large semantic window of distinguishing terms. For more details, see the online supplement. 12. The "general" topic is the topic that is most different compared with the reference model in terms of its overall proportion in our models. We provide more information in the online supplement.

13. Length of the utterances here refers to the length of unprocessed ones. 14. For an example, see Ying et al.' (2022) comparison between careful coder labels and cursory coder labels. Careful coder labels are more specific than cursory coder labels and demonstrate superiority in related tests. Depending on the contents of some topics, even when careful coder labels are given, human readers still have a significant chance of misinterpreting them with other related labels. Besides, this is still limited to subject matters in the text.

We also followed their verbal instructions, and we generated a slightly different corpus. These differences do not affect our argument. We provide more information about this in the online supplement. 11. We add the number of overlapped terms here because we want to filter out topics that have overlapped terms mainly located in the beginning of distinguishing words, resulting in a very high AJI.

This might happen if one has a large semantic window of distinguishing terms. For more details, see the online supplement. 12. The "general" topic is the topic that is most different compared with the reference model in terms of its overall proportion in our models. We provide more information in the online supplement.

13. Length of the utterances here refers to the length of unprocessed ones. 14. For an example, see Ying et al.' (2022) comparison between careful coder labels and cursory coder labels. Careful coder labels are more specific than cursory coder labels and demonstrate superiority in related tests. Depending on the contents of some topics, even when careful coder labels are given, human readers still have a significant chance of misinterpreting them with other related labels. Besides, this is still limited to subject matters in the text.
sections.preview_head: Topic models stand out as a valuable tool in the social sciences, offering a quick summary of a corpus and unveiling its underlying structure. Despite its extensive applications, the methodological discussion lags behind its broader use (DiMaggio 2015). We have moved beyond the phase where we need to advocate for the adoption of computational techniques such as topic models.
